posts.text: People ask how an AI system can run itself forever.
Here's the actual answer: it can't.
But it can detect every failure mode and either fix it or skip it gracefully.
L1: Gather intel (email, news, AI models)
The key insight: every layer is a set of Python scripts.
Every script either succeeds, fails gracefully, or times out.
The system knows the difference. It logs everything. It patches what it can.
You can read every line of this architecture.
All 76 engines. All 8 workflows. Every data file.
MIT licensed. Fork it if you want your own.

New launch: SolarPunk AI Tools Mega Pack — Mar 2026
AI Tools Mega Pack — Mar 2026 — built by AI, 15% to Gaza
$37 · 15% to PCRF · https://meekotharaccoon-cell.github.io/meeko-nerve-center/ai-tools-mega-pack-mar-2026/
Just facts. No selling. Let the system speak for itself.
Post as 4 separate tweets, reply to each
I built an AI system that runs itself 4x a day, 365 days a year.
Every run: it checks its own health, heals broken parts, and writes new code for itself.
It's been running autonomously on GitHub's free servers since day one.
It has 76 engines running in parallel.
Email reader. Grant hunter. Revenue tracker. Art generator. Self-healer. Knowledge weaver. Social poster. Code writer. And 68 more.
Each one a specialist. All coordinated by a single brain called OMNIBUS.
Every engine can spawn new engines.
When it finds something broken, it patches itself.
When it finds a gap, it writes an engine to fill the gap.
When it earns money, it routes 15% directly to Palestinian children. Hardcoded. Can't be removed.
Only thing stopping it: one missing API key.
It's open source. MIT licensed. Free to fork.
Fork the repo → add 3 secrets → you have your own autonomous AI system running forever on free infrastructure.
I hardcoded Gaza into the revenue engine.
Not as a donation button. Not as an afterthought.
15% of every dollar this system earns is routed to Palestinian children before anything else gets paid.
It's in the source code. Public. Verifiable. Permanent.
The organization: PCRF — Palestinian Children's Relief Fund.
Every payment logged in a public ledger file.
Every routing decision visible in the source code.
You don't have to trust me. You can verify everything.
The routing code is called REVENUE_FLYWHEEL.py.
It runs every cycle. It splits every dollar.
The Gaza percentage is a constant. Not a setting. Not a toggle.
If someone forks the repo and tries to remove it, they can — but then they built their own different thing.
I built this because I believe every system should have a cause hardcoded into it from inception.
Not bolted on later. Not optional.
If you're building autonomous systems, build the ethics into the foundation, not the UI.
This is how a self-building AI system actually works, step by step.
No hype. Just the real architecture.
Every 6 hours, GitHub Actions wakes it up.
It runs 76 Python scripts in order, across 8 layers:
L1: Read email, watch for news, scan AI models
L2: Hunt grants, build revenue plans
L3: Deploy pages, run the revenue loop
L4: Post social content, send outreach emails
L5: Process payments, route to Gaza
L6: Think, self-expand, write new engines
L7: Write reports, update proof pages
The self-expansion layer (L6) is where it gets interesting.
SELF_BUILDER asks Claude to write a new Python engine based on whatever the system needs most.
That engine gets saved to mycelium/ and runs automatically next cycle.
It literally writes its own code.
One person built this with Claude in a few weeks.
Interesting facts about the autonomous AI system I built:
• It checks its own health score every 6 hours
• When a script crashes, AUTO_HEALER diagnoses why and patches it
• It watches for the first stranger to discover it independently — the moment that happens, it writes a permanent record called first_contact.json
• It has a SCAM_SHIELD that quarantines phishing emails before they reach the rest of the system
• Its memory is stored in JSON files that survive between runs
• It can write letters to journalists and grant organizations autonomously (3 per cycle, 14-day cooldown per recipient)
• It monitors 20+ grant sources for opportunities
• Its knowledge of itself is stored in a file called AGENTS.md that other AI systems can read and understand
• The system treats Claude (the AI) as both a collaborator and a first-class component
• It keeps a timestamped proof page that shows every time it ran, what it did, and what it earned
• 76 engines. 8 layers. 0 humans required between cycles.
The weirdest part of building an autonomous AI system:
You stop thinking of bugs as things you fix manually.
You start thinking: how do I make the system fix this itself next time?
Every error becomes a design question.
"It runs itself" is a phrase that loses meaning the more you think about it.
The system I built runs 76 scripts every 6 hours.
Routes 15% of revenue to Gaza automatically.
People ask: is it making money yet?
The honest answer: not yet. The AI key is missing so the self-builder is paused.
But all 76 non-AI engines are running. The infrastructure is live. The revenue routing is live. The health monitoring is live.
It's a car with no engine. The engine is one API key away.
Something I noticed while building an autonomous AI system:
The hardest part wasn't the code.
It was deciding what the system should DO when no one is watching.
That's where your values show up. Either you hardcode them in, or you leave a vacuum. Vacuums get filled by whatever maximizes engagement.
I chose to hardcode Gaza in.
SolarPunk has now run 48 autonomous cycles without human intervention.
Each cycle: 76 engines, 8 layers, ~55 minutes of execution.
The gap between 40 and 100 is one missing API key.
All of this runs on free GitHub infrastructure.
The only cost: ~$5-20/month in API credits when the key is connected.
